summary: The post explores techniques for optimizing Python code that is bound by data structure access rather than numerical operations, using a benchmark representative of Dask's task scheduler. Working with Cython developer Stefan Behnel, Rocklin tests two optimization strategies: compiling with Cython (without type annotations) and interning strings for faster dictionary lookups. The combined approaches yield a 2-3x speedup on the toy benchmark, while PyPy performs even better at 4-5x faster. However, when applied to an actual Dask scheduler benchmark, none of these optimizations showed meaningful improvement, demonstrating the gap between toy benchmarks and real-world complexity.
key_points: Cython can provide modest speedups (30%) even for data-structure-bound Python code without type annotations, though far less than the 10-100x gains typical for numerical code
String interning improves dictionary lookups by allowing Python to use fast identity comparisons instead of full string equality checks
PyPy significantly outperforms Cython on data-structure-heavy workloads, achieving 4-5x speedups over baseline CPython
The Dask scheduler can run under PyPy while clients and workers use standard CPython for PyData compatibility
Toy benchmarks often fail to represent real application performance - the optimizations that worked on the simple benchmark showed no effect on actual Dask scheduler code
money_quotes: This is an atypical problem Python optimization because it is primarily bound by data structure access (dicts, lists, sets), rather than numerical operations commonly optimized by Cython.
Python is already decently fast here, typically within a factor of 2-5x of compiled languages like Java or C++, but still we'd like to improve this when possible.
This isn't huge relative to typical Cython speedups (which are often 10-100x) but would be a very welcome change for our scheduler, where we've been chasing 5% optimizations for a while now.
If you ensure that there is only one copy of every string then you only need to do identity comparisons like text is text.
So PyPy can be quite a bit faster than Cython on this sort of code (which is not a big surprise).
The Dask scheduler is significantly more complex and it is difficult to build a single toy example to represent its performance.
key_insight: While Cython, string interning, and PyPy can significantly optimize data-structure-bound Python code in isolation, real-world applications like Dask's scheduler are too complex for simple benchmarks to predict actual performance gains.
